The free cash flow reached a solid $1.82 billion (slightly below the $1.93 billion seen in 2024), as the drop in CAPEX helped offset the macro pressure to some degree, while the year also came with the major acquisition of LesserEvil, growing their exposure to the high-growth better-for-you snack category.

HSY also announced unifying its sweet, salty and protein portfolios into ONE, which they say would help bring more innovation, flavors, variety, etc., combining the execution of their commercial activity and centralizing their global brand marketing.

As for the guidance, the company expects a solid 4% to 5% growth in net sales (with about 150 b.p. coming from the LesserEvil acquisition), with the Adjusted EPS growing by 30% to 35% (mostly thanks to a mark-to-market loss unwinding, margin recovery and a normalization of tax effects), while CAPEX would reach between $425 million to $475 million, similar to last year's $454.62 million.

Financially, based on HSY's latest report, they remain in a solid position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities, and even though the debt is growing to fund their expansion, their maturities remain spread very well, with over $925 million in cash and cash equivalents offering a lot of flexibility.

Given the potential for macro improvements and the solid balance sheet, we can certainly see how their idea of 2026 being a year to build back margins can be true, with more detail to come during their Investor Day at the end of March:

(...) we are balancing our commitment to achieving the full profit potential of our business with our focus on long-term sustainable growth and strong margins. In 2026, you will see us increase our budgets for R&D, innovation, brand building and our retail sales team. We will talk more about these and expected returns at our Investor Day in March. Advertising and related consumer spending is expected to increase double digits versus the prior year as we support momentum across our business. Investment will be up across both working and nonworking media as we develop creative in support of our long-term brand and portfolio objectives.

The company's ~2.73% dividend was recently increased by 6% compared to a year ago, representing about $1.15 billion, meaning a payout ratio of roughly 63% based on last year's cash flow, which is not bad, but also not an amazing yield compared to others in the industry.

Mixed Macro Signals

The recent Supreme Court decision (regardless of the increase to 15% that followed it) was a solid development overall, potentially helping the environment and offering some relief for the consumer from the pressure caused by the higher tariff rates, with the previous exemption reportedly carrying over and helping cocoa prices continue their decline, although they're still trading at relatively elevated levels.

That’s important especially given the significant levels of consumer pressure, which caused trouble in the US in recent years even for companies that were considered much more defensive, which came as a result of a mix between the ongoing high-rate environment combined with these tariffs and now the Iran conflict.

We can already see the significant jumps in gas prices, and this conflict can certainly lead to a lot worse the longer it lasts for, which can be terrible for HSY and its consumers.

The latter already caused the Fed to warn about the uncertainty seen in the economy, recently deciding not to cut the rates and signaling the potential of a single rate cut later this year, causing even more fear for an already weak market.

Long-Term Risks Persist

However, although Hershey has some of the most popular brands in their categories, we should keep in mind that these trade-downs and macro pressure can still affect their brands, as retailers might be the biggest long-term winners from this thanks to their private label brands.

Not only can they gain the trust of consumers and build these brands as a result of the significant trade-downs, but they also gather excellent amount of valuable data that can allow them to tailor their portfolio based on consumer trends, potentially capturing the consumer's demand for higher price points when the consumer's budget recovers (e.g. launching a Premium/Deluxe/Supreme/etc. version of the in-house brand).

This goes together with the potential impact from GLP-1 medicine, which was shown to lead to drops of ~10% in grocery spending for certain sweets and snacks, which can affect Hershey at its core, with the company recently calling it a potential demand headwind.

Still, given their strong brands and potential to focus on higher-protein, lower-calorie and other trends combined with the fact that the adoption of this medicine will realistically not be 100%, I don't expect the impact to be something they can't manage, and especially given their potential to expand internationally, I believe they can potentially more than mitigate these risks, which are likely going to be much bigger threats for lower-price-point products that can be easily replaced.

However, this can also be accentuated by HSY's moves (likely) in an effort to preserve/improve their margins to the detriment of their quality, which Brad Reese, the grandson of the famous recipe's inventor also highlighted recently. It's true that the current environment is tough for everyone and they aren't the only ones downgrading/replacing ingredients or “right-sizing” their offering, but it's these practices that make consumers gradually lose their trust, which can have detrimental effects during a potential recovery and in the longer term.

Valuation

For HSY’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start from last year’s $1.82 billion FCF and assume a CAGR of 8% for the first 5 years (meaning ~$1.97 billion in 2026) thanks to the potential macro improvements and margin developments, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Note that I won't adjust for net borrowings since their portfolio remains in a solid state, without needing to do significant adjustments.

I’m going to use a conservative discount rate of 9.5% to highlight the competitive risk and potential fundamental pressure but also balanced by their solid financials and brands, with 203.27 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $36.40 billion, meaning an intrinsic value of $179.08 per share, which continues to be above the current $210.58.

Conclusions

HSY remains a Hold, as even though their financials remain solid given the cash flow and balance sheet, the ongoing macro developments are far from great, while their quality concerns combined with other fundamental threats should make investors demand a higher margin of safety than the current levels.
